\chapter{Vendor/MSP \& CRM Lifecycle}

\index{Vendor/MSP \& CRM Lifecycle}

This part demystifies how vendors and managed service providers handle the customer journey from first outreach to renewal. Students learn how CRM platforms coordinate sales commitments...

\section{Challenger Sales Mindset}
\index{Challenger Sales Mindset}
Many IT deals die because reps chase people who can’t say yes. With long procurement cycles and security reviews, smart qualification keeps you from camping in inboxes that never reply. Exactly. A Challenger mindset means starting with a data-backed insight that proves you understand their business. That opens the door to ask sharper questions about budget, authority, and technical fit.

And it shows non-IT folks you can translate tech into outcomes that matter in any industry. When insight and qualification work together, you save everyone time and move opportunities forward.

\subsection{Challenger approach}

\index{Challenger approach}

Picture an IT director drowning in help-desk tickets. Instead of pitching faster hardware, you show how a self-service portal could cut requests by 30\%. That's the Challenger approach—teach a new perspective tied to their KPI, then steer toward a pilot so momentum doesn't stall. It's like being a consultant who actually knows what they're talking about. By taking control of the conversation, you prevent deals from lingering in "maybe" land.

And you build credibility because you're leading with insight and value, not desperation or discounts.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Teach customers a new perspective
\item Tailor the message to their priorities
\item Take control of the conversation and next steps
\item Reframe with data (self-service portal cuts tickets by 30\%)
\item It's like being a consultant who actually knows what they're talking about
\end{itemize}

\subsection{BANT qualification basics}

\index{BANT qualification basics}

I once chased a "hot" lead from a marketing manager who swore they had \$50K. After five demos, spending froze until next fiscal year. A BANT question like "Who signs the PO and when is budget released?" would have saved those calls. BANT covers Budget, Authority, Need, and Timeline. Red flags appear when answers stay vague or your contact dodges finance. New reps often mistake enthusiasm for authority.

Ask, "What happens if this waits a quarter?" If they shrug, move on before your forecast becomes a ghost story.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Budget available and defined ("We have \$50K" but funds arrive next year)
\item Authority to approve the purchase — talking to someone's nephew who 'handles the computers' won't close deals
\item Need clearly articulated — ask what happens if it waits a quarter
\item Timeline for decision and rollout
\end{itemize}

\subsection{MEDDIC framework}

\index{MEDDIC framework}

MEDDIC sounds like a prescription drug, but skip a step and the deal still hurts. In IT, a security tool may need sign-off from risk, legal, and finance before a check is cut. So map Metrics, Economic buyer, Decision criteria, Decision process, Identify pain, and find a Champion. Ask, "What metrics matter to leadership?" and "Who loses sleep if this fails?" If your contact can't introduce the economic buyer, they're an influencer, not the decision maker.

And without a named pain and champion pushing internally, procurement becomes a black hole.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Metrics that define success
\item Economic buyer who holds the funds (engineer $\ne$ CIO)
\item Decision criteria and process
\item Identify pain points and a champion
\item MEDDIC sounds like a prescription drug, but it won't cure your sales hangover
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Using frameworks together}

\index{Using frameworks together}

The magic happens when Challenger insight sets the stage and BANT or MEDDIC confirms the show should go on. You might reframe a hospital’s slow admissions as a data issue, then verify the COO has budget this quarter. In IT or retail, the pattern is the same—teach, tailor, take control, then qualify. Red flags like "we’re just exploring" signal the pain isn’t urgent.

These frameworks also help non-sales roles prioritize projects or vendors. They’re universal filters for where to invest energy, whether you’re closing software deals or choosing a cybersecurity internship.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with insight to reframe the problem
\item Qualify opportunities with BANT or MEDDIC
\item Map findings to your value proposition
\item Works in IT and beyond to focus energy on real deals
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Whether you're selling software or pitching an internal project, smart qualification protects your time and credibility. Chasing unvetted leads burns trust and budgets across every industry. This week, review your pipeline or class assignments. List Budget, Authority, Need, and Timeline for each, then map any missing MEDDIC pieces. If gaps remain, schedule discovery calls, escalate to a champion, or move prospects to nurture instead of clogging forecasts.

Mastering this discipline shows hiring managers you think like a strategist, not a spray-and-pray rep, and it keeps colleagues from muttering about wasted meetings.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Leading with insight and structured qualification drives consistent deal success.
\item Next step: map one current lead using BANT and MEDDIC.
\end{itemize}


\section{Communication Protocols}
\index{Communication Protocols}
This section sets up Communication Protocols. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{Why establish protocols?}

\index{Why establish protocols?}

Communication is the oil that keeps vendor relationships running smoothly. Right, without a plan those check-in calls either never happen or spiral into unproductive chats. Setting expectations early prevents scrambling later when an incident hits. We'll cover how to schedule regular meetings, when to escalate and what reports to exchange so everyone's in the loop.

With a bit of structure you spend less time chasing updates and more time actually solving problems.

\subsection{Regular check-ins}

\index{Regular check-ins}

Regular check-ins keep projects aligned as priorities shift. I like setting a recurring calendar invite so nobody forgets. Share an agenda ahead of time so the call stays focused. Review metrics and open action items, then flag any roadblocks coming up. Even a brief weekly sync helps catch small issues before they turn into crises. Think of it as routine maintenance for the partnership.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Schedule weekly or monthly calls depending on service criticality
\item Share agendas beforehand and track action items
\item Review ticket metrics, upcoming releases and potential risks
\item Allocate time for roadblocks or resource requests
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Escalation paths}

\index{Escalation paths}

Escalation paths map out who to call when something goes wrong. Without them, you end up in voicemail jail while the outage drags on. Define levels of severity and the expected response time for each. Make sure both sides know the direct line to a manager if the normal channel fails. Clear paths mean faster resolutions and less finger pointing.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Define primary contacts and backups at both companies
\item Document severity levels and response times
\item Provide a direct line to management for urgent issues
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Reporting cadence}

\index{Reporting cadence}

Reporting cadence keeps everyone accountable. Monthly performance summaries show if service levels are slipping. Incident reports within a day help us learn what went wrong and how to prevent repeats. Quarterly reviews give space to adjust long-term plans or budgets. Frequent but predictable updates stop surprises from derailing projects.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Monthly performance summaries and quarterly strategy reviews
\item Incident reports within 24 hours of outages
\item Periodic satisfaction surveys to adjust course
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Structured communication may feel formal, but it saves headaches later. When everyone knows the schedule and who to call, problems get solved quickly. So set up your meetings, document escalation contacts and keep sharing reports. Do that and your vendor relationships will run a lot smoother.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Consistent communication builds trust, keeps projects on track and provides metrics to gauge vendor responsiveness and issue resolution.
\end{itemize}


\section{Competitive Displacement Strategies}
\index{Competitive Displacement Strategies}
Most prospects already have a vendor in place. Winning the deal often means replacing that incumbent. Competitive displacement proves your solution fixes pain the current one ignores. Price alone rarely wins; you must show why switching is worth the hassle. It's like convincing someone to switch mobile phone providers—they need clear benefits to overcome the hassle.

Give them a reason to say the effort is worth it.

\subsection{What is displacement?}

\index{What is displacement?}

What is displacement? focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Replacing an incumbent vendor with your solution, Requires solving pain points the competitor misses, and Common where markets are saturated and contracts lock clients in. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Requires solving pain points the competitor misses; Common where markets are saturated and contracts lock clients in.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Replacing an incumbent vendor with your solution
\item Requires solving pain points the competitor misses
\item Common where markets are saturated and contracts lock clients in
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Research the incumbent}

\index{Research the incumbent}

Step one is studying the incumbent vendor—the tool already running the show. And don't just read their marketing; reviews, support forums and even job ads reveal the real pain points. Maybe customers gripe that ServiceNow tickets take three days for a first response. That's your opening to highlight a four-hour SLA. Dig into switching costs and integrations to show you understand their reality.

It proves you're not just selling, you're solving problems they already feel.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item "Incumbent vendor" = the tool or provider already in use
\item Analyse competitor features, pricing and support reputation
\item Gather customer complaints and reviews to find weaknesses
\item If ServiceNow tickets take 3 days for first response, showcase your 4-hour SLA
\item Map internal champions and existing relationships
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Targeted differentiation}

\index{Targeted differentiation}

Now that you've spotted the incumbent's weak spots, tailor your pitch to hit them directly. Saying "we're better" is vague; showing a feature that saves hours is persuasive. Transparent pricing and ROI numbers help buyers justify the switch. Nobody wants hidden fees popping up like airline baggage charges at check-in. Side-by-side comparisons make it easy for them to sell the idea internally.

You're not just different—you're different in ways that matter. That's how you replace an entrenched competitor.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Lead with unique value that addresses known pain
\item Provide clear ROI and transparent pricing
\item Use side-by-side comparisons to highlight advantages
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Structured migration plan}

\index{Structured migration plan}

Even with a great pitch, migration anxiety can stall the deal. Bring a clear plan—timelines, tools and who does what. Explain data migration as moving records, tickets and user accounts to the new system. Think of swapping Zendesk for ServiceNow using CSV exports, field mapping guides and a three-month parallel run. Offer pilots or phased rollouts with proof-of-concept testing so they can validate without risk.

Set expectations that enterprise swaps often take six to twelve months. Provide training and migration support to ease the transition. When the path is mapped out, disruption fears fade and momentum builds.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Outline steps, timelines and resources for moving off the old platform
\item Explain data migration: moving records, tickets and user accounts
\item Offer tooling, data migration support and training
\item Example: from Zendesk to ServiceNow with CSV exports, field mapping and a 3-month parallel run
\item Set realistic timelines—enterprise swaps often take 6-12 months
\item Reduce risk with pilots or phased rollouts and proof-of-concept testing
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Land and expand}

\index{Land and expand}

Big wins usually start small. Target a single team where success will be visible. Maybe launch in HR ticket management and track a 50\% faster resolution time. Those numbers make it easy to expand into IT operations. Quick wins create internal champions who vouch for you. It's a land-and-expand play instead of an all-or-nothing bet. Momentum beats massive launches that never get off the ground.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Start with a foothold project or department
\item Demonstrate quick wins then broaden adoption
\item Example: begin with HR ticket management, then move to IT ops after 50\% faster resolutions
\item Track success metrics to justify expansion
\item Maintain champions to secure renewal
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Ethics and professionalism}

\index{Ethics and professionalism}

It's tempting to trash the incumbent, but that can backfire. Buyers respect vendors who focus on their own strengths. Avoid FUD—spreading fear, uncertainty and doubt about the other guy. Honour existing contracts and confidentiality agreements. No one wants a partner who plays dirty. Professionalism builds trust long after the ink dries. Ethical wins are the ones that stick.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Emphasise your strengths rather than spreading FUD (Fear, Uncertainty, Doubt)
\item Respect contract terms and confidentiality
\item Build trust so displacement feels like partnership
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Roles and career paths}

\index{Roles and career paths}

Different roles tackle displacement from different angles. Sales engineers prove the tech works and design the migration path. Customer success managers handle stakeholder politics once the pilot lands. Solution architects plan how the platform will scale across departments. Juniors gather pain points and coordinate demos. Senior staff weave the pieces together and keep the long game in sight.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales Engineers validate solutions and design migrations
\item Customer Success Managers manage stakeholder relationships
\item Solution Architects plan long-term expansions
\item Junior reps surface pain points; senior staff orchestrate the swap
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Displacement works when you solve a real problem and make change feel safe. Do the homework, show clear value and keep the process ethical. In crowded markets that's the difference between a lost bid and a flagship customer. Win on merit, deliver on promises and the switch will stick.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Successful displacement solves real pain, eases migration risk and stays ethical.
\end{itemize}


\section{Contract Negotiation Basics}
\index{Contract Negotiation Basics}
This section sets up Contract Negotiation Basics. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{Why negotiate carefully}

\index{Why negotiate carefully}

Why negotiate carefully focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: We have all seen boilerplate vendor contracts. Why not just accept them?, Speaker B: Remember the Black Friday payment system outage we mentioned earlier? If you sign standard terms, you might find those "industry standard" uptime guarantees are worthless, and Speaker A: So we press for clear uptime commitments, detailed service levels, and the ability to exit if things go south.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Remember the Black Friday payment system outage we mentioned earlier? If you sign standard terms, you might find those "industry standard" uptime guarantees are worthless; Speaker A: So we press for clear uptime commitments, detailed service levels, and the ability to exit if things go south; Speaker B: Exactly.

A well-negotiated contract tells the vendor how quickly they must respond, how they'll compensate you, and what recourse you have if service fails.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: We have all seen boilerplate vendor contracts. Why not just accept them?
\item Speaker A: So we press for clear uptime commitments, detailed service levels, and the ability to exit if things go south.
\item Speaker B: Exactly. A well-negotiated contract tells the vendor how quickly they must respond, how they'll compensate you, and what recourse you have if service fails.
\item Speaker A: It also locks in pricing so you don't face huge renewal hikes.
\item Speaker B: Negotiation sounds tedious, but think of it as building a safety net before you walk the tightrope.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Typical SLA terms}

\index{Typical SLA terms}

Typical SLA terms focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Uptime numbers look impressive, but let's translate them into real impact. 99.9\% uptime allows about 8.7 hours of downtime a year; 99.99\% cuts that to under an hour, Speaker B: Picture your ecommerce store offline for a full business day during peak season. That's more than an inconvenience—it's lost revenue, and Speaker A: Good SLAs also clarify incident notification and escalation paths.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Picture your ecommerce store offline for a full business day during peak season. That's more than an inconvenience—it's lost revenue; Speaker A: Good SLAs also clarify incident notification and escalation paths; Speaker B: Plus, penalties or credits if the vendor misses targets.

"Best effort" or "reasonable attempts" is code for "we'll try, maybe.".

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker B: Picture your ecommerce store offline for a full business day during peak season. That's more than an inconvenience—it's lost revenue.
\item Speaker A: Good SLAs also clarify incident notification and escalation paths.
\item Speaker B: Plus, penalties or credits if the vendor misses targets. "Best effort" or "reasonable attempts" is code for "we'll try, maybe."
\item Speaker A: Security and data handling should meet your compliance needs. If customer information leaks, you may face fines before the vendor even apologizes.
\item Speaker B: Strong SLAs keep daily operations running smoothly and give you leverage when they don't.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Pricing models}

\index{Pricing models}

Pricing models focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Let's talk money. Vendors offer flat fees, per-user pricing, or pay-as-you-go models, Speaker B: The last one can be surprising. One startup projected \$500 a month on AWS but got a \$5,000 bill during a holiday rush, and Speaker A: Compare that with a fixed \$2,000 a month hosting plan—expensive on quiet days, but stable during busy times.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: The last one can be surprising. One startup projected \$500 a month on AWS but got a \$5,000 bill during a holiday rush; Speaker A: Compare that with a fixed \$2,000 a month hosting plan—expensive on quiet days, but stable during busy times; Speaker B: Ask about volume discounts, yearly increases, and onboarding fees.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: Let's talk money. Vendors offer flat fees, per-user pricing, or pay-as-you-go models.
\item Speaker B: The last one can be surprising. One startup projected \$500 a month on AWS but got a \$5,000 bill during a holiday rush.
\item Speaker A: Compare that with a fixed \$2,000 a month hosting plan—expensive on quiet days, but stable during busy times.
\item Speaker B: Ask about volume discounts, yearly increases, and onboarding fees.
\item Speaker A: And watch out for seemingly minor charges, like mandatory training or premium support.
\item Speaker B: It's like buying a cheap printer and discovering the ink costs a fortune. Do the math for best and worst-case scenarios so you know what you're signing up for.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Exit and renewal options}

\index{Exit and renewal options}

Exit and renewal options focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Ever heard of a company stuck in a contract for six months after service went downhill?, Speaker B: Unfortunately yes, because they needed 180 days' notice to terminate. Always read the exit clause, and Speaker A: Look for data portability promises too. When vendor X went bankrupt, company Y lost three months of data because extraction wasn't covered.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Unfortunately yes, because they needed 180 days' notice to terminate. Always read the exit clause; Speaker A: Look for data portability promises too. When vendor X went bankrupt, company Y lost three months of data because extraction wasn't covered; Speaker B: Renewal terms deserve equal attention.

Automatic price increases can sneak up on you if you don't have time to renegotiate.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: Ever heard of a company stuck in a contract for six months after service went downhill?
\item Speaker B: Unfortunately yes, because they needed 180 days' notice to terminate. Always read the exit clause.
\item Speaker A: Look for data portability promises too. When vendor X went bankrupt, company Y lost three months of data because extraction wasn't covered.
\item Speaker B: Renewal terms deserve equal attention. Automatic price increases can sneak up on you if you don't have time to renegotiate.
\item Speaker A: Treat the exit plan like a fire drill. You hope you never need it, but you'll be glad it's there in a crisis.
\item Speaker B: A clear renewal calendar and exit path keep you in control rather than locked in.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Risk assessment frameworks}

\index{Risk assessment frameworks}

Risk assessment frameworks focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: How do you know if a vendor is risky? Start by checking financial stability—are they profitable or bleeding cash?, Speaker B: Then examine security posture. When was their last penetration test and how quickly do they patch?, and Speaker A: Frameworks like NIST's vendor risk assessment or the SIG questionnaire provide structured questions.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Then examine security posture. When was their last penetration test and how quickly do they patch?; Speaker A: Frameworks like NIST's vendor risk assessment or the SIG questionnaire provide structured questions; Speaker B: Score areas such as finance, security, and support history.

If they fall below your threshold, reconsider.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: How do you know if a vendor is risky? Start by checking financial stability—are they profitable or bleeding cash?
\item Speaker B: Then examine security posture. When was their last penetration test and how quickly do they patch?
\item Speaker A: Frameworks like NIST's vendor risk assessment or the SIG questionnaire provide structured questions.
\item Speaker B: Score areas such as finance, security, and support history. If they fall below your threshold, reconsider.
\item Speaker A: This process isn't just bureaucracy. Think of it as weather forecasting before a hike; you want to know if a storm is coming.
\item Speaker B: Document the results and revisit them annually. Circumstances change, and so should your risk profile.
\end{itemize}

\item Speaker A: Multi-vendor setups add complexity. If your CRM is hosted by one provider and your analytics by another, who takes the blame when integrations break?
\item Speaker B: Clear contracts specify responsibilities so you avoid finger-pointing later.
\item Speaker A: Matching the delivery model to your business needs ensures you get the right support without paying for unnecessary extras.
\item Speaker B: Consider how these models will evolve as the business scales.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Integration requirements}

\index{Integration requirements}

Integration requirements focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Have you confirmed the vendor's system actually works with your existing tools?, Speaker B: Check API access, data exchange formats, and authentication methods, and Speaker A: A vendor might claim "it integrates with everything" only for you to learn that "everything" means their other paid products.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Check API access, data exchange formats, and authentication methods; Speaker A: A vendor might claim "it integrates with everything" only for you to learn that "everything" means their other paid products; Speaker B: Clarify upfront whether you'll need custom development, middleware, or extra licenses.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: Have you confirmed the vendor's system actually works with your existing tools?
\item Speaker B: Check API access, data exchange formats, and authentication methods.
\item Speaker A: A vendor might claim "it integrates with everything" only for you to learn that "everything" means their other paid products.
\item Speaker B: Clarify upfront whether you'll need custom development, middleware, or extra licenses.
\item Speaker A: Poor integration can turn a simple project into a costly headache, especially if data needs to flow in real time.
\item Speaker B: Document these requirements in the contract so there's no dispute later.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Scalability and growth planning}

\index{Scalability and growth planning}

Scalability and growth planning focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: What happens when your business triples in size?, Speaker B: Make sure the contract allows for scaling up—or down—without punitive fees, and Speaker A: Include options for additional capacity, more users, or even moving to a larger service tier. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next.

Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: Make sure the contract allows for scaling up—or down—without punitive fees; Speaker A: Include options for additional capacity, more users, or even moving to a larger service tier; Speaker B: I've seen companies caught off guard by data growth charges that skyrocketed after a successful marketing campaign.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: What happens when your business triples in size?
\item Speaker B: Make sure the contract allows for scaling up—or down—without punitive fees.
\item Speaker A: Include options for additional capacity, more users, or even moving to a larger service tier.
\item Speaker B: I've seen companies caught off guard by data growth charges that skyrocketed after a successful marketing campaign.
\item Speaker A: Also plan for shrinking usage if business slows; you shouldn't pay for unused capacity.
\item Speaker B: Scalability clauses protect you from outgrowing the vendor or wasting money when demand drops.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

The key takeaway is this: Speaker A: Negotiating a contract is about more than price, Speaker B: A well-crafted agreement covers uptime, pricing, exit strategies, and how the service will grow with you, and Speaker A: Use structured risk assessments and thorough checklists to evaluate vendors. Use that takeaway to name the owner, evidence, and next action that should be visible after the work is done.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: Negotiating a contract is about more than price.
\item Speaker B: A well-crafted agreement covers uptime, pricing, exit strategies, and how the service will grow with you.
\item Speaker A: Use structured risk assessments and thorough checklists to evaluate vendors.
\item Speaker B: Bring in legal expertise early, especially for international or regulated industries.
\item Speaker A: Keep the relationship healthy with regular reviews and training, and be ready to pivot if performance slides.
\item Speaker B: Good contracts form partnerships that survive growth, change, and the occasional curveball.
\end{itemize}


\section{Cost Optimisation Strategies}
\index{Cost Optimisation Strategies}
This section sets up Cost Optimisation Strategies. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{Why cost management matters}

\index{Why cost management matters}

Like gym memberships, software licences are easy to sign up for and surprisingly hard to cancel. Surveys suggest ten to twenty percent of IT budgets pay for tools no one uses—what finance calls shelfware. Over-provisioning is another culprit: paying for more capacity than you need just in case. Even a ten-person startup can uncover savings with a simple spreadsheet sweep.

Cost optimisation pulls finance, procurement and IT ops into the same conversation so those leaks get spotted early. A quarterly review keeps renewals from creeping up unnoticed. And a little humour helps when you ask, "Do we really need 300 video meeting seats when half the team still thinks the cloud is weather?" Redirecting that wasted spend funds projects that actually matter.

\subsection{Usage analysis}

\index{Usage analysis}

Start by pulling usage reports for every major service. If you pay for one hundred Office 365 licences but only sixty accounts show activity, that's forty cancellations waiting to happen. Engineers call this over-provisioning—paying for capacity you never touch. Shelfware is software bought but never used at all. A simple spreadsheet with columns for licence count, cost, owner and last login date reveals both problems quickly.

Review it at least quarterly and share highlights with finance and procurement. Those numbers become your negotiation superpower because they show exactly where spend doesn't match value. Without data you are guessing; with data you steer the conversation.

\subsection{Common cost traps}

\index{Common cost traps}

Once you start digging, common cost traps pop up everywhere. Auto-escalating pricing clauses bump rates a few percent every year without anyone noticing. Paying per user instead of per active user leaves phantom licences attached to people who left months ago. Teams might be running Slack, Teams and Discord simultaneously because no one made a decision. I've also seen three different monitoring dashboards all watching the same servers.

My favourite discovery is the test environment scaled like production and humming away every night and all weekend. Each issue looks tiny on its own but multiplies quickly. A simple quarterly checklist—prices, active users, duplicate tools and idle servers—keeps these traps from draining the budget. Fixing them is often as easy as an email or a shutdown script.

\subsection{Renegotiation timing}

\index{Renegotiation timing}

Vendors love auto-renewals because they lock in last year's price without a fight. Set calendar reminders ninety days before renewal so you have time to gather data and options. Show up with specifics: "Our usage dropped thirty percent since remote work ended, so we're downgrading a tier." Ask about discount brackets, bundling services or paying per active user.

Remember, account managers have quotas too—they want to keep you as a customer. Sometimes offering a longer commitment trades flexibility for lower cost, which is fine if forecasts are stable. You're not begging for a favour; you're aligning spend with reality. And if the vendor won't budge, you still have time to explore alternatives before the auto-renewal kicks in.

\subsection{Tactics and frameworks}

\index{Tactics and frameworks}

Beyond timing, a toolbox of tactics keeps spending in check. Begin each quarter with a checklist covering licence counts, usage trends, upcoming renewals and any overlapping tools. Keep a template of negotiation questions like, "What discount tiers exist?" or "Do you offer per active user pricing?" For a quick ROI calculation divide the cost of the tool by the hours saved and multiply by the hourly rate.

Cloud bills shrink when you schedule batch jobs for off-peak hours and purchase reserved instances, which are prepaid blocks of capacity cheaper than on demand. Practised together, these habits turn cost optimisation into a repeatable business process instead of a once-a-year scramble.

\subsection{Practical exercise: case study}

\index{Practical exercise: case study}

Let's practise with a simple case. Imagine a startup paying for Slack, Teams and Discord while half its test servers run all weekend. Estimate how much is wasted each month: maybe a thousand dollars on chat apps and another thousand on idle compute. Could consolidating tools or scheduling nightly shutdown scripts recapture that spend? Write down two actions you'd take and which teams you'd involve—IT ops for automation, finance for the numbers, procurement for contract changes.

Exercises like this build the muscle of spotting waste and rallying the right people to fix it before budgets get cut.

\subsection{Careers in cost optimisation}

\index{Careers in cost optimisation}

Who actually owns cost optimisation in a company? It's shared across procurement analysts who negotiate contracts, IT operations staff who track usage and FinOps specialists who blend finance with technical know-how. Entry level roles might start with licence audits or cleansing billing data. With experience you can move into vendor manager, cloud economist or IT finance manager positions.

Useful skills include spreadsheet fluency, a curiosity about how systems work and the confidence to challenge a vendor's pricing. If you're the person friends ask to split the dinner bill, you might enjoy turning those instincts into a career.

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Costs rarely stay flat without effort. Even a small business can find savings; retiring two unused apps might cover the team lunch budget. Regular reviews, awareness of common traps and a simple negotiation playbook keep budgets under control. Treat vendor spend like any other investment—measure, question and adjust. Keep a sense of humour too; you might discover you're paying for five hundred licences when half the office thinks the cloud is just weather.

Savings from these audits can fund security upgrades or that innovation project you've been pitching. Cost optimisation isn't about being cheap; it's about making sure every dollar pulls its weight.


\section{CRM Fundamentals}
\index{CRM Fundamentals}
This section begins with CRM fundamentals—where customer relationship management meets IT operations. If you've ever wondered how tech companies keep track of thousands of customers across sales, support, and success teams, you're in the right place. Think of a CRM as the central nervous system for customer interactions. Every support ticket, sales conversation, and renewal discussion flows through it.

And thanks to Salesforce's free Trailhead platform, you can experiment in a safe playground without touching production data. By the end of this session you'll see why CRM literacy helps whether you're triaging incidents, planning rollouts, or guiding customers toward long-term success.

\subsection{What is a CRM?}

\index{What is a CRM?}

Picture this: a customer calls your help desk frustrated because their software isn't working. Without a CRM, the support rep starts from scratch—"What's your account number? What product do you use? Have you contacted us before?" With a CRM, the rep instantly sees the customer's full history: previous tickets, known issues, contract details, even which sales engineer handled the implementation.

It's like having a shared memory that never forgets and is available to sales, support, and success teams. When everyone works from the same record, nobody drops the ball and customers feel heard instead of repeating themselves.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item System to track customer data and interactions
\item Stores contact info, interaction history, support tickets, purchase history
\item Supports sales, service and marketing teams
\item Single source of truth across departments
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Core Salesforce objects}

\index{Core Salesforce objects}

Salesforce organises data into standard objects that fit together like LEGO blocks. Accounts represent companies; Contacts store the people at those firms; Leads capture raw interest from marketing; and Opportunities track potential revenue. Suppose a lead from a tech expo becomes qualified. It converts into an Account named "TechCorp Inc." with Contacts like "John Smith, CTO" and "Sarah Lee, IT Manager." The sales team then opens an Opportunity to follow the deal through demos, proposals, and contracts.

Keeping these relationships clean prevents the messy desk syndrome where vital details vanish under piles of sticky notes.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Accounts hold company info
\item Contacts track people at those accounts
\item Leads capture potential interest
\item Opportunities represent active deals
\item Example: Account "TechCorp Inc." → Contacts "John Smith (CTO), Sarah Lee (IT Manager)"
\end{itemize}

\subsection{CRM in IT organizations}

\index{CRM in IT organizations}

CRM isn't just for salespeople. In many IT organisations, the support desk lives in the same system so agents can see who the customer is, what infrastructure they run, and which SLAs apply before they even say hello. When incidents escalate, the CRM record links to the ITIL ticket, showing prior outages, open change requests, and the account's tier. That context helps engineers prioritise fixes and communicate appropriately.

It's far better than hunting through inboxes like a messy desk for missing details. Aligning CRM and service management keeps technical teams customer aware and prevents surprises during critical calls.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Support desks see account details before answering
\item Links tickets to customer SLAs and past incidents
\item Guides escalation paths and handoffs
\item Integrates with ITIL processes
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Trailhead modules to start}

\index{Trailhead modules to start}

Salesforce Trailhead is like a free gym membership for your CRM muscles. Start with CRM Basics to learn terminology, then move into Leads and Opportunities to practice qualifying prospects and tracking deals. The Accounts \& Contacts trail shows how relationships are mapped, while Reports \& Dashboards teaches you to spot trends without needing a data scientist.

None of these modules turn you into a salesperson any more than learning Excel makes you an accountant, but they make you more valuable to any team. Each badge is a mini project you can show on your résumé or LinkedIn profile.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Salesforce CRM Basics
\item Leads and Opportunities
\item Accounts \& Contacts
\item Reports \& Dashboards for Lightning Experience
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Hands-on practice}

\index{Hands-on practice}

The best way to learn is by doing. Spin up a Trailhead Playground and experiment with creating accounts, logging support tickets, and running reports without fear of breaking anything. Guided projects walk you through real scenarios like converting a lead or building a dashboard for open incidents. You can replay steps until they stick and even explore API tools if you're feeling adventurous.

Treat the playground as your lab bench—practice the workflow, make mistakes, and document what you learn so you can bring that discipline back to production environments.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Complete modules in a Trailhead Playground
\item Use guided projects to apply features
\item Experiment safely without touching production
\item Earn badges to show progress
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

So the takeaway is simple: mastering CRM basics gives you a shared language with sales, support, and customer success. It's like tidying that messy desk so everyone can find the right file when the phone rings. Trailhead lets you build those skills for free, and the concepts map back to ITIL practices like incident tracking and change management. Whether you end up in sales engineering, technical account management, or service delivery, understanding the customer record keeps you aligned to business outcomes.

Keep exploring modules and you'll quickly see how powerful an organised CRM can be.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Trailhead provides a free path to learn CRM concepts by doing.
\end{itemize}


\section{Customer Success Teams}
\index{Customer Success Teams}
The deal is finally signed, everyone high-fives, and then what? Does the customer magically become successful? Not even close. That's when customer success steps in, the team that babysits adoption long after the sales team packs up the confetti. So they're like a pit crew, tuning the car after the big race, except the race never really stops. Exactly, and Monday morning starts with dashboards and check-ins rather than a hunt for the next prospect.

Makes sense—selling software is easy compared with getting people to actually use it. Which is why we'll spend the next few minutes exploring how these teams keep customers happy and revenue growing.

\subsection{Why customer success matters}

\index{Why customer success matters}

Plenty of projects look great on launch day and then quietly fizzle. Why bother with a customer success manager at all? Because they catch those fizzles before they turn into cancellations. A good CSM calls before the login numbers drop, not after. Kind of like a fitness coach texting you when you've skipped the gym for a week. Right, plus they ferry feedback back to product teams—"Hey, three clients tripped over the same API limit." So they're part detective, part ambassador.

And when renewal time arrives, they've already proven the software paid for itself, which beats a desperate last-minute pitch.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Post-deployment value delivery: 73\% of software purchases fail to deliver expected ROI without a CS team guiding adoption
\item Churn prevention: Proactive engagement cuts churn by 67\% compared with reactive support models
\item Voice of customer: Success managers feed real usage data back to product and engineering to shape roadmaps
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Driving adoption}

\index{Driving adoption}

So once the CSM is on board, how do they nudge people to actually use the product? They start with a playbook—kickoff calls, training sessions, and a champion who rallies their coworkers. Like handing out workout plans instead of just giving someone a gym card. Exactly, and they watch the telemetry. If feature usage flatlines or support tickets spike, they jump in with office hours or tutorials.

Have you seen it work? Slack's team ran weekly workshops for Acme Corp and turned a ghost town into 80\% active users in half a year.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Onboarding playbooks outline training sessions, champion networks, and milestones so users aren't left staring at blank logins
\item Health checks review login frequency, feature usage, and ticket volumes to spot stalled deployments early
\item Best-practice libraries and success plans act like personal trainers—buying the gym membership isn't enough without a workout schedule
\item Example: Slack's CS crew coached Acme Corp through weekly workshops and workflow templates, taking team adoption from 20\% to 80\% in six months.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Collaboration \& health metrics}

\index{Collaboration \& health metrics}

Once adoption's on track, is the CSM done? Hardly. That's when they look for new value. Maybe the client loves reporting but hasn't tried automation yet. Upsell time? More like matchmaking. They connect usage gaps to business goals and suggest features that solve real pains. Any examples? Gainsight flagged a yellow health score for a fintech client. The CSM introduced workflow automation and closed a 30\% expansion before renewal even came up.

Sounds less pushy, more advisor. Exactly—helping customers win is the best sales pitch.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Cross-functional partnerships with sales, product, and support keep everyone aligned on customer outcomes
\item Health scores mix login frequency, feature depth, and support ticket trends; "How are you feeling?" doesn't scale to 10,000 accounts
\item Early warnings: Red scores trigger escalation paths before renewal time turns into a crisis
\item Example: A drop in Net Promoter Score at HubSpot prompted CS to partner with support and ship tutorials, restoring scores within one cycle.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Expansion and renewals}

\index{Expansion and renewals}

All that tracking must take a lot of cooperation across teams. It does. CS shares usage trends with product, flags billing risks for sales, and pings support when tickets spike. And those "health scores" everyone talks about—what's actually in them? Usually logins, feature depth, survey results, even how many tickets a client raises. It's a bit like checking pulse, blood pressure, and mood in one snapshot.

Handy, because asking "How are you feeling?" doesn't scale to thousands of customers. Exactly, and when a score dips, there's a playbook ready before renewal panic sets in.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Upsell and cross-sell opportunities surface when CS maps product usage to business goals and uncovers new pains
\item Renewal timelines are tracked months in advance with risk flags and stakeholder maps
\item Churn prediction models weigh executive sponsorship, adoption metrics, and sentiment to prioritise outreach
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Customer success tools}

\index{Customer success tools}

Those playbooks sound organised. What tools keep it all straight? Gainsight and ChurnZero are the big ones—dashboards for health scores, tasks, even automated emails. So a CSM starts Monday by scanning those dashboards like pilots checking instruments. Exactly. Salesforce or HubSpot then house the success plans and meeting notes. And support data? Often piped in from Zendesk or Intercom so the CSM sees trouble tickets without digging.

Sounds like a lot of systems. It is, but integrating them gives the team superpowers: no surprises and fewer frantic “any updates?” calls.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Gainsight and ChurnZero aggregate usage analytics, playbook tasks, and renewal dashboards that a CSM checks first thing Monday
\item Salesforce and HubSpot host success plans and document the handoff from sales to post-sales teams
\item Specialised add-ons like Zendesk or Intercom surface support signals directly in the success console
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Handoff \& playbooks}

\index{Handoff \& playbooks}

How do deals move from sales to customer success without dropping details? Ideally there's a formal handoff meeting. Sales shares the promised outcomes, key contacts, and any skeletons in the closet. Then CS runs with it? With support close by. They agree on who tackles technical issues versus adoption coaching, often using a RACI chart. So no one argues later about "I thought you were doing that." Exactly.

A clear playbook means the customer feels a single smooth journey instead of a baton dropped between teams.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Sales to CS: Deals arrive with documented business goals, admin contacts, and promised features—no "surprise" requirements
\item CS to support: Escalation runbooks and RACI charts clarify who tackles technical hiccups vs adoption coaching
\item Onboarding playbooks spell out week-by-week tasks, from kickoff calls to data import dry runs
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Careers in customer success}

\index{Careers in customer success}

This sounds like a different breed of IT job. What career options exist in customer success? Plenty. You have CSMs managing accounts, Technical Success Managers helping with APIs, and analysts crunching usage data. Where do these folks come from? Many start in support or account management and pivot. Relationship builders with some technical curiosity do well.

What about pay and growth? Entry roles hover around seventy grand, senior managers can hit six figures, and directors lead large teams with strategic sway. So it's a path for people who like both tech and people. Exactly, a hybrid career where empathy meets analytics.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Roles range from Customer Success Manager and Technical Success Manager to data-savvy CS Analyst
\item Entry pathways often start in account management, support, or consulting; strong communicators with curiosity thrive
\item Salary ranges: entry-level roles around USD \$70k, seasoned managers \$100k–\$150k, with director paths beyond
\item Skills blend relationship building, light project management, and enough technical aptitude to translate logs into plain English
\item Example: A former support engineer moves into a Technical Success role, leverages API know-how, and progresses to managing strategic accounts within two years.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

So the big picture is clear—selling the software is just the opening act. Right, the real value shows up when customer success guides adoption, measures health, and loops in other teams. And they do it with playbooks, metrics, and even a bit of humor when health scores go orange. Plus there's a solid career path for anyone who likes helping people and digging into data.

Sounds like customer success turns one-time deals into long-term stories. Exactly. When the handoffs are smooth and the tools are humming, renewals feel like a celebration, not a cliff edge.


\section{Discovery Call Techniques}
\index{Discovery Call Techniques}
Many reps treat discovery calls as mini demos, but the point is to uncover the client's real struggles. I learned that after a prospect sat through my entire slide deck and then said their real issue was a three-hour daily reboot cycle. Exactly. When we focus on curiosity instead of pitch, clients open up about things like legacy servers that crash every Monday or a "temporary" security patch that's been in place for a year.

Framing the call as a joint investigation sets a collaborative tone. You're not there to sell yet; you're there to understand why their help desk ticket volume doubled or why their cloud bill keeps creeping up. And when they realize you're genuinely trying to solve the puzzle, not just push a product, they start treating you like a partner rather than another vendor with a quota to hit.

\subsection{Set the stage}

\index{Set the stage}

Doing a bit of homework lets you skip basic questions and quickly establish credibility. A quick, totally professional LinkedIn stalk tells you if they're expanding, downsizing, or just posted about a security scare. Then set a simple agenda: where they are now, what's in the way, and what a good outcome looks like. In virtual calls, share it in chat so no one gets lost in the tabs.

I also check cultural cues. A client in Tokyo may expect a longer warm-up, while a New Yorker wants to dive in. Either way, a warm opener like "How are you keeping your remote team connected?" gets them talking. That question alone once uncovered a firm running four different chat tools. By the time we got to solutions, they were practically begging for a unified platform.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Research prospect and context – LinkedIn stalking, but make it professional
\item Open with agenda and outcome
\item Use open-ended warm-up questions
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Probe for pain points}

\index{Probe for pain points}

Open-ended prompts like "What's slowing your team down?" invite them to vent about the real issues. I spent years asking yes-or-no questions and wondering why the conversations went nowhere. And if there's a pause, sit with it—count to five, sip your coffee. People hate silence and often fill it with the good stuff, like how server downtime is costing them \$10k an hour or how their CRM still doesn't talk to the help desk.

One client finally admitted they copy-paste data between five systems every morning. That awkward pause exposed a \$50k problem they hadn't even quantified. Another confessed audit season turns into "spreadsheet panic week" because their security controls are duct-taped together. None of that shows up if you're busy pitching instead of probing.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Ask what's slowing them down today
\item Explore current workaround or cost (server downtime, audit panic)
\item Let silence work – embrace the awkward pause
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Listen and dig deeper}

\index{Listen and dig deeper}

When they describe a problem, paraphrase it back; it shows you're listening and can reveal gaps. "So your remote engineers lose half a day waiting for VPN access—did I get that right?" Follow up with "What happens if nothing changes?" I joke, "You'll keep having these calls forever," and the chuckle breaks tension while the reality sinks in. Listening also means watching for cues.

On video, a glance at another screen might signal they're checking an internal chat about budget. In person, folded arms can mean you've hit a nerve. Dig gently with questions like "How are you handling that security breach aftermath?" The more they share, the clearer the path to a solution, whether it's cloud migration help or a new remote-work policy.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Paraphrase to confirm understanding
\item Ask "what happens if it stays this way?" – you'll keep having these calls forever
\item Note emotional cues and urgency (breach aftermath, remote-work headaches)
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Common discovery mistakes}

\index{Common discovery mistakes}

The easiest way to tank a discovery call is to talk more than the prospect. I once filled ten minutes explaining our stack and realized they hadn't said a word beyond "hello." Pitching too early is another trap. If you demo backup software before they admit their last ransomware scare, you're solving a problem they haven't owned yet. The third mistake is skipping follow-up questions.

When a client mentions their cloud migration is "messy," don't nod and move on. Ask, "Messy how?" You might uncover a remote office still running a closet server under someone's desk. Avoid these pitfalls and the call feels like a consult, not a commercial. You learn where they're bleeding time or money, and they feel heard instead of sold to.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Talking too much
\item Pitching too early
\item Not asking follow-up questions
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Qualify and guide next steps}

\index{Qualify and guide next steps}

Once pain is clear, check who else needs to weigh in and what timeline they're working to. "If we solved the VPN bottleneck by quarter's end, who signs off and what milestones matter?" Tie their problem to the value you can bring. "Reducing those 3-hour reboot windows could give your team a full extra day each week." Now they see dollars, not features. Before you hang up, agree on a concrete next step.

I like to schedule the demo while energy is high and promise a follow-up email summarizing what we heard. That recap isn't just polite; it proves you're organized and gives them something to forward internally. It's how a tentative chat about cloud migration turned into a multi-site roll-out for one of my clients.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Check timeline, budget and authority
\item Link pain to potential value quickly
\item Schedule follow-up while energy is high
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Discovery isn't about having the perfect pitch. It's about being curious enough to uncover pains worth solving and proving you can help. When you ask thoughtful questions, listen for what's unsaid, and dodge the common mistakes, prospects start sharing the messy stuff—like the remote office still on Windows 7 or the compliance audit that keeps them up at night.

Wrap each call by recapping what you heard and the next step you both agreed on. That simple follow-up note shows respect for their time and keeps the momentum going. Do that consistently and discovery calls stop feeling like cold outreach. They become the start of real partnerships that solve real problems.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Curious, patient questions uncover the real problems you can solve.
\end{itemize}


\section{Key Economics in Tech Sales}
\index{Key Economics in Tech Sales}
This section sets up Key Economics in Tech Sales. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{ARR vs one-time deals}

\index{ARR vs one-time deals}

You know how Netflix charges every month? ARR is that idea applied to business software. Right—take all the subscription contracts and imagine them as steady monthly income. Salesforce flaunts over thirty billion in ARR, while a consulting shop lives off project spikes. Investors love that predictability; one-off services cash just doesn't get the same respect.

\subsection{Benefits of ARR}

\index{Benefits of ARR}

Predictable revenue lets finance plan headcount and infrastructure. Sales reps can focus on expansions instead of hunting for entirely new customers. Multiples for SaaS firms are often based on ARR, so growing it boosts valuation. It also tightens relationships because customers expect ongoing support and upgrades.

\subsection{When one-time deals make sense}

\index{When one-time deals make sense}

One-off projects still matter, like a hospital's compliance audit or a hardware rollout. It's like being a wedding photographer—great pay per gig, then you're hunting the next bride and groom. Without a subscription, account managers start from zero at the beginning of each fiscal year. Comparisons get messy when large one-time invoices land in different reporting periods.

\subsection{Pricing models}

\index{Pricing models}

Pricing models focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Per-seat licences scale with headcount but can cap expansion, Usage-based fees map cost to consumption, common for fintech APIs, and Tiered feature bundles let customers graduate as needs grow. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Usage-based fees map cost to consumption, common for fintech APIs; Tiered feature bundles let customers graduate as needs grow; The pricing mix shapes upsell paths and revenue predictability.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Per-seat licences scale with headcount but can cap expansion.
\item Usage-based fees map cost to consumption, common for fintech APIs.
\item Tiered feature bundles let customers graduate as needs grow.
\item The pricing mix shapes upsell paths and revenue predictability.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Contract structures}

\index{Contract structures}

Contract structures focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Annual terms give frequent renewal checkpoints, Multi-year deals lock in revenue but may require discounts, and Up-front prepay boosts cash flow; quarterly payments ease budgets. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Multi-year deals lock in revenue but may require discounts; Up-front prepay boosts cash flow; quarterly payments ease budgets; Auto-renew clauses and payment terms influence churn risk.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Annual terms give frequent renewal checkpoints.
\item Multi-year deals lock in revenue but may require discounts.
\item Up-front prepay boosts cash flow; quarterly payments ease budgets.
\item Auto-renew clauses and payment terms influence churn risk.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Land-and-expand strategy}

\index{Land-and-expand strategy}

Land-and-expand starts with a small deal to prove value. Slack often began with a hundred-seat pilot before spreading to thousands of users. Customer success plays a key role by spotting use cases and advocating upgrades. The model reduces risk for buyers while giving vendors a pathway to larger contracts.

\subsection{Metrics to watch}

\index{Metrics to watch}

Metrics reveal whether the model is working. Track churn and customer lifetime value to see if clients stick around. Net retention over one hundred percent means expansions beat churn. LTV to CAC and payback period—basically, how many dates before this relationship pays for dinner.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item ARR and MRR show the recurring base; ACV measures a contract's annual value.
\item Gross retention under 90\% signals churn problems; net retention over 100\% shows healthy expansion.
\item Track customer lifetime value, LTV:CAC ratios and expansion revenue from cross-sell or upsell.
\item Payback period asks how many months before acquisition cost is recovered.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Practical exercise: revenue mix}

\index{Practical exercise: revenue mix}

Imagine a company with five hundred thousand in ARR and two hundred thousand in services. If twenty percent of that services work became subscription, ARR would rise to five hundred forty thousand. How would that change valuation or hiring plans? Sketch the numbers in a spreadsheet to see how recurring revenue smooths the curve.

\subsection{Careers in revenue economics}

\index{Careers in revenue economics}

Understanding these economics opens doors beyond quota-carrying sales roles. In fintech or healthcare, revenue ops analysts juggle usage spikes and compliance churn. Sales finance partners build models to justify headcount or pricing changes. Customer success managers tie renewals and expansions to product adoption.

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

Whether you're selling or supporting, knowing how revenue flows shapes better decisions. ARR provides stability, one-time deals give spikes, and land-and-expand balances both. Mastering these concepts helps align day-to-day tactics with long-term growth. In tech sales, economics is as important as product features.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Understanding how ARR, one-time deals and land-and-expand motion shape cash flow helps align sales tactics with long-term growth.
\end{itemize}


\section{Lead Scoring \& Renewal Signals}
\index{Lead Scoring \& Renewal Signals}
This section sets up Lead Scoring \& Renewal Signals. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{Why scoring and alerts matter}

\index{Why scoring and alerts matter}

Picture an account executive juggling twenty live deals and forty inbound leads. Without prioritisation, you'll spend an hour demoing to someone whose budget is "whatever's in the coffee fund" while the enterprise buyer who's ready to sign goes straight to your competitor. Lead scoring ranks prospects so the CRM surfaces the conversations most likely to close.

The same discipline applies to renewals—alerts on usage drops or contract anniversaries give time to intervene. When you combine both, the CRM stops being a passive database and becomes an active workflow coach for the revenue team.

\subsection{Lead scoring fundamentals}

\index{Lead scoring fundamentals}

We split scoring signals into explicit and implicit. Explicit means facts about the lead—industry, company size, job title, tech stack compatibility. Implicit signals show intent, like downloading the pricing guide, attending a webinar or requesting a sandbox. Marketing and sales must agree on what score equals "marketing-qualified" so hand-offs are automatic, not debate material.

Document those definitions inside the CRM fields and playbooks so new hires follow the same rules from day one.

\subsection{Building the scoring model}

\index{Building the scoring model}

Start with a basic formula rather than an opaque AI model. Maybe ten points for being in your ideal customer profile, five for a director or above, fifteen for booking a demo. Deduct points for students, partners researching you for their own clients or anyone outside target regions. Just remember—if you give too many points for downloading brochures, your sales team will be chasing every student doing research for their assignment.

Every quarter, compare scores on closed-won deals versus lost deals and adjust the weights. Transparency matters—if reps understand why a lead is 85 points, they will trust the automation and act quickly.

\subsection{Data privacy and compliance}

\index{Data privacy and compliance}

Data privacy and compliance focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Lead scoring must respect consent. Document lawful bases, honour unsubscribe preferences and minimize access to personal data. Configure GDPR or CCPA fields in the CRM and ensure synced tools update or delete records when prospects opt out. Trust erodes faster than any score if privacy rules are ignored.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next.

\subsection{Integration challenges}

\index{Integration challenges}

Integration challenges focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Scoring shines when marketing automation, product usage and support systems sync cleanly. Expect mismatched fields, duplicate records or differing unique IDs. Use middleware or iPaaS tools, enforce naming conventions and schedule data-quality audits so automation fires on reliable signals instead of stale or conflicting data.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next.

\subsection{Opportunity stage progression}

\index{Opportunity stage progression}

Scoring gets leads into the pipeline, but stage definitions keep deals moving. Agree on exit criteria like "Qualified means we know budget, authority, need and timeline". When a proposal is sent, log the mutual action plan or next meeting date so leadership can coach instead of guess. Accurate close dates and next steps make pipeline reviews collaborative rather than uncomfortable interrogations.

It also feeds more reliable forecasts to finance, which keeps the business trusting the sales org.

\subsection{Automation and task queues}

\index{Automation and task queues}

Automation stops opportunities falling through the cracks. When a lead crosses the marketing-qualified threshold, auto-create an opportunity and assign the right account executive. Mid-score leads can drop into nurture sequences that drip education until intent spikes again. But what if the automation creates busy work? That's why you review triggered tasks monthly—automation should create qualified opportunities, not just more emails to ignore.

If a deal stalls longer than its normal stage duration, trigger a task or manager alert so someone re-engages. Systems handle the nudges; humans focus on quality conversations.

\subsection{Testing and tuning scoring models}

\index{Testing and tuning scoring models}

Testing and tuning scoring models focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Pilot new scoring formulas with a subset of leads, comparing conversion, velocity and rep feedback. Alternate threshold values or point weights in structured experiments so marketing and sales can prove which model accelerates pipeline without overwhelming teams. Document findings and iterate like any other growth experiment.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next.

\subsection{Renewal alerts and health signals}

\index{Renewal alerts and health signals}

Renewals deserve the same rigour as new business. Tag every account with contract start dates, renewal deadlines and notice periods so alerts fire in advance. Pull usage, NPS and support data into the CRM to colour-code health—green, amber, red. Monthly customer success reviews on the 120, 90 and 60-day lists give plenty of time to resolve issues or plan upsells.

It feels much better to call with a success plan than to apologise after a surprise cancellation.

\subsection{Churn prediction models}

\index{Churn prediction models}

Churn prediction models focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Move beyond binary alerts by combining product usage, support sentiment and contract value into predictive models. Use machine learning or rules-based health scores to forecast churn probability, then route high-risk accounts for executive reviews or value-add campaigns. Always balance prediction accuracy with interpretability so teams know which levers to pull.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next.

\subsection{Metrics to track}

\index{Metrics to track}

Metrics prove whether the process works. Track lead-to-opportunity conversion and compare the average score of deals you win versus those you lose. Stage aging reports show where deals stall so you can refine exit criteria or coaching. Those are a lot of numbers to track—which ones matter most to leadership? Renewal retention rate and forecast accuracy tell finance whether to trust the numbers coming from Salesforce dashboards.

And marketing needs visibility so campaign targeting mirrors the behaviour of leads who actually convert. Close the loop by sharing a simple scorecard that highlights wins, risks and the actions each team should take next.

\subsection{Careers and collaboration}

\index{Careers and collaboration}

Who owns all this plumbing? Usually revenue operations. RevOps analysts translate between marketing automation, sales processes and customer success playbooks. Marketing operations specialists curate the scoring logic while customer success managers interpret renewal health signals. Entry-level coordinators might clean data, build dashboards or test workflows before stepping into manager roles.

Picture a data coordinator cleaning duplicate leads, then designing scoring models as a Marketing Operations Specialist and later orchestrating the entire tech stack as a Senior RevOps Manager. Curiosity about buyer behaviour and the ability to facilitate cross-team workshops are the career superpowers here.

\subsection{Common pitfalls}

\index{Common pitfalls}

Common pitfalls focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Over-engineering scoring models that sales teams do not trust, Setting renewal alerts too late for meaningful intervention, and Tracking metrics that fail to drive clear action plans. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Setting renewal alerts too late for meaningful intervention; Tracking metrics that fail to drive clear action plans.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Over-engineering scoring models that sales teams do not trust
\item Setting renewal alerts too late for meaningful intervention
\item Tracking metrics that fail to drive clear action plans
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Key takeaway}

\index{Key takeaway}

The goal isn’t a flashy dashboard, it’s predictable growth. Keep the scoring model simple, enforce stage hygiene and schedule renewal reviews like clockwork. Automate the reminders but regularly sanity-check them against real conversations. When data, process and people stay aligned, the CRM becomes the nervous system of revenue operations. That discipline is what separates teams who scramble at quarter end from those who hit plan consistently.


\section{Legislation and SLA Compliance}
\index{Legislation and SLA Compliance}
This section sets up Legislation and SLA Compliance. Treat it as the frame for the decisions, handoffs, and evidence that appear in the next slides. The practical question is simple: by the end, what should a junior IT professional be able to explain, check, or document in a real workplace?

\subsection{Why legislation matters in SLAs}

\index{Why legislation matters in SLAs}

Why legislation matters in SLAs focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Before we negotiate uptime percentages, let's talk about what happens when things go wrong, Speaker B: We keep talking about "strong SLAs". Why drag privacy law into a commercial contract?, and Speaker A: Because regulators do not care that a vendor missed its target.

If personal data leaks, the customer of record is still on the hook. In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: We keep talking about "strong SLAs". Why drag privacy law into a commercial contract?; Speaker A: Because regulators do not care that a vendor missed its target.

If personal data leaks, the customer of record is still on the hook; Speaker B: So we use the contract to push legal obligations onto the vendor?.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: Before we negotiate uptime percentages, let's talk about what happens when things go wrong...
\item Speaker B: We keep talking about "strong SLAs". Why drag privacy law into a commercial contract?
\item Speaker A: Because regulators do not care that a vendor missed its target. If personal data leaks, the customer of record is still on the hook.
\item Speaker B: So we use the contract to push legal obligations onto the vendor?
\item Speaker A: Exactly. SLAs need clauses that align with statutory duties, otherwise fines and breach notifications land on us.
\item Speaker B: Think of legislation as the non-negotiable baseline before we even discuss uptime numbers.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Australia’s Privacy Act 1988 (APP 11)}

\index{Australia’s Privacy Act 1988 (APP 11)}

Australia’s Privacy Act 1988 (APP 11) focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: What does APP 11 actually require from a SaaS provider?, Speaker B: It demands reasonable steps to secure personal information and to destroy or de-identify it once it is no longer needed, and Speaker A: Remember the Medibank hack—9.7 million records exposed. APP 11 "reasonable steps" failed and class actions north of \$50M followed.

In practice, ask who owns the work, what evidence proves it happened, and what handoff comes next. Use the supporting details as a checklist: Speaker B: It demands reasonable steps to secure personal information and to destroy or de-identify it once it is no longer needed; Speaker A: Remember the Medibank hack—9.7 million records exposed. APP 11 "reasonable steps" failed and class actions north of \$50M followed; Speaker B: In an SLA we convert that into encryption standards, 30-day breach notifications and secure disposal guarantees.

\paragraph{Practice checkpoints.}

\begin{itemize}[leftmargin=*]
\item Speaker A: What does APP 11 actually require from a SaaS provider?
\item Speaker B: It demands reasonable steps to secure personal information and to destroy or de-identify it once it is no longer needed.
\item Speaker A: Remember the Medibank hack—9.7 million records exposed. APP 11 "reasonable steps" failed and class actions north of \$50M followed.
\item Speaker B: In an SLA we convert that into encryption standards, 30-day breach notifications and secure disposal guarantees.
\item Speaker A: We also need audit rights to prove those "reasonable steps" exist. Without evidence, we cannot defend ourselves to the OAIC.
\item Speaker B: Include joint incident response drills so vendors are not improvising when something goes wrong.
\end{itemize}

\subsection{Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018}

\index{Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018}

Security of Critical Infrastructure Act 2018 focuses attention on a concrete part of the work. Speaker A: Some clients insist on Critical Infrastructure Act coverage.
